result:
# The Weight of Beginning

The hardest part is always the first step. Not because it requires the most effort — that distinction usually belongs to the middle, to the long slog where novelty has worn off and the end remains out of sight. No, the first step is hardest because it is the most consequential. It declares direction. It eliminates possibilities. Before you step, you could go anywhere. After, you can only go forward or back.

I've been thinking about this lately, about how much of my life has been spent standing at thresholds. Doorways I wouldn't walk through. Conversations I wouldn't start. Pages I wouldn't fill. There's a kind of safety in the pause before beginning, a temporary immortality — as long as you haven't started, you haven't failed. The project remains perfect in the realm of imagination, untainted by the messy compromise of execution.

But here's what I've learned, slowly, reluctantly, the way you learn most things that matter: the mess is the point. The compromise is where life actually lives.

---

My grandfather kept a garden. Not a picturesque garden, not the kind that appears in magazines with trimmed hedges and color-coordinated beds. His was a rambling, chaotic quarter-acre behind his house in Baltimore, tomatoes intermingling with marigolds, squash vines creeping across the lawn like they were trying to escape, a rusted swing set repurposed as a trellis for beans. He went out every morning at six, regardless of weather, and worked until his back told him to stop.

I asked him once why he planted so much when it was just him and my grandmother. He couldn't eat all of it. Half of it rotted on the vine or got eaten by insects or simply bolted in the July heat. He looked at me like the question didn't make sense.

"Things grow," he said. "That's what they do. I just show up."

Just show up. I've spent years turning that phrase over in my mind, looking for its secret, convinced there had to be more to it. But I think the secret is that there isn't more to it. The secret is that showing up is the whole thing. The rest is just weather and time.

---

I started writing during a winter when I couldn't seem to do anything else. I'd lost a job — not dramatically, just the quiet kind of ending where a contract isn't renewed and you realize you've been sitting at a desk for three years without once feeling necessary. The days shortened. I woke in darkness and sat at the kitchen table with a notebook and tried to put one word after another.

What surprised me wasn't that it was hard. I expected it to be hard. What surprised me was how the difficulty changed character over time. In the beginning, it was the difficulty of starting — the blank page's accusation, the feeling that everything had already been said by people who said it better. But after a few weeks, that particular monster grew tired. It still showed up, but it napped more often. And in its absence, I found a different difficulty: the difficulty of being honest.

You can write without being honest. People do it all the time. You can construct sentences that are grammatically correct, even beautiful, that say nothing true about your experience of being alive. You can hide behind style, behind irony, behind the safe distance of the third person. And the work might even be good. But it won't be yours. Not really. It will be a performance of writing rather than the thing itself.

The thing itself requires you to say what you actually mean, to admit when you're afraid, to name the thing you've been avoiding. It requires you to show up, as my grandfather would say, and to keep showing up even when what you find when you arrive is uncomfortable.

---

There's a concept in woodworking called "reading the grain." Before you cut a piece of wood, you study the direction of its fibers, the subtle currents running through its body. If you cut against the grain, the wood splinters and resists. If you cut with it, the blade passes through like it's moving through water. The wood was always going to go that way. You just had to learn to see it.

I think people have grain too. Not fate, exactly — I don't believe our paths are predetermined. But there are directions we're shaped to move, currents in us that were formed long before we had language for them. The child who took things apart to see how they worked grows into the engineer. The child who made up stories grows into the writer. Not because they had no choice, but because moving with the grain is easier than moving against it, and the things that come easily to us are often the things that matter most.

The trouble is that our grain isn't always visible. It's covered over by expectations, by fear, by the accumulated debris of years of trying to be what someone else needed. Finding it requires patience and a willingness to make wrong cuts, to splinter the wood a few times before you understand its structure.

---

I'm thinking now about what I want to say to you, whoever you are, reading this. I don't know your circumstances. I don't know what threshold you're standing at or how long you've been standing there. But I suspect you know what it means to hesitate. I suspect you've felt the weight of unmade choices, the gravity of a life still waiting to be lived.

So here is what I have, which is not advice exactly, because I'm not qualified to advise anyone. It's more like a report from the road:

The first step doesn't have to be right. It just has to be taken. You can adjust. You can change direction. But you can't adjust from a standstill. Movement creates the possibility of correction; stillness creates only the illusion of safety.

Show up. Read the grain. Start before you're ready.

The perfect page is the one you haven't written yet. Write it anyway.

message.reasoning:
1000 words. Whatever comes to me.

Let me think about what wants to be written. I'll just start and see where it goes.

I think I'll write something reflective, maybe about the nature of starting things, about the blank page, about the strange space between intention and creation. Let me just write and see where it leads.
